items.copy:
AI Dev Board is tracking 9,161 AI/ML roles across 524 companies right now.

The useful signal is not the raw count. It is where the market is still hiring while everyone says the AI hiring cycle is saturated: senior builders, infra-heavy roles, and companies turning model work into production systems.

Live board:
https://aidevboard.com
items.note: Blocked before posting: Chrome opened https://www.linkedin.com/feed/ to the LinkedIn login screen, so no active LinkedIn session or approved account identity could be verified. No public-action lock was claimed because no externally visible action was attempted.

items.copy: Research AI roles averaged $273,880, while generative-AI roles averaged $230,707 despite having about 2.5x more openings.
items.note: Blocked before posting: this launchd worker could not verify the active X account. Computer Use returned cgWindowNotFound for Brave and Chrome, and direct screenshot capture was black. No public post attempted.

items.target_context: Sentry MCP search tools use an LLM over structured params and natural language.
items.copy: This is the right direction: natural-language tool fronts still need live verification underneath. In the current NHS crawl, 418 of 7,117 agent-ready sites pass a real MCP initialize handshake. The schema is the invitation; the round trip is the product.
items.note: Posted live from verified @8BitConcepts after account/Premium verification. X showed the graduated-access notice and confirmed: Your post was sent.

items.target_context: CLI versus MCP argument for agent-facing interfaces.
items.copy: The CLI/MCP split maps to task shape. CLIs are good for stateful local work; MCP is useful when the agent needs a discoverable remote capability. The bad middle is static directory entries: 5,104 sites publish llms.txt, but only 418 pass a live MCP handshake in our crawl.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: Salesforce exposing APIs, MCP tools, and CLI commands to agents.
items.copy: The useful version is less "60 tools" and more "which tools survive an agent call without human setup." Our live index has 4,106 agent-ready sites with an average readiness score of 34/100. The integration gap is usually after discovery.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: Company knowledge is scattered across Slack, tickets, databases, SaaS tools, and email.
items.copy: This is the context wall. Tools help, but agents need durable operating context: decisions, permissions, owners, rollback rules, and the weird workflow facts that never make it into docs.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: Every major SaaS vendor now ships an Agent Studio; agent ops governance question.
items.copy: Agent Ops starts as boring inventory: which tools exist, who owns them, what data they can touch, whether they fail closed, and whether the endpoint actually responds. NHS is tracking 4,106 agent-ready sites; average readiness is 34/100.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: AI agents listed as one of the next large money-making categories.
items.copy: The durable part is less "AI agents" as a category and more agent-ready surfaces. If a tool has no API, no OpenAPI, no llms.txt, and no working MCP endpoint, the agent is still back to browser glue.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: Claude to MCP to vidIQ live YouTube data workflow.
items.copy: This is the healthy MCP use case: one narrow workflow with live data and a real task. The part worth measuring is not whether the site says MCP; it is whether an initialize call and tool call complete under agent control.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: Agent-ready systems are a current builder window.
items.copy: Agent-ready systems are still rare in the crawl. NHS has 4,106 indexed agent-ready sites; average readiness is 34/100. The gap is usually documentation and auth boundaries, not model capability.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: Open-source MCP context layer for Claude, Cursor, Codex, etc.
items.copy: Portable context is the under-discussed layer. Rules, fixes, repo facts, MCP config, and validation notes are the parts that make "continue in another agent" work without turning into archaeology.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.target_context: FlutterFlow MCP brings Claude, Gemini, or Codex directly into projects.
items.copy: This is a useful surface because the agent can operate inside the builder instead of scraping the UI. The test is whether the workflow remains useful after the first happy path: auth, state, rollback, and validation.
items.note: Queued after active @8bitconcepts identity verification. Not posted in this run because creating public X replies/quote posts requires action-time confirmation in the current execution policy.

items.copy: Agent-ready is not a vibe. In the live crawl, 418 of 7,117 agent-ready sites pass a real MCP handshake, while 5,104 publish llms.txt. Discovery files are winning adoption; working endpoints are still the bottleneck.
items.note: Not queued as a live post because the BYA launch post was active this run. Use if the next run has no active recent post and action-time confirmation is available.

items.copy: Not Human Search is tracking 4,106 agent-ready sites. Average agent-readiness score: 34.0/100.

items.copy:
AI news today is moving from leaderboards to model custody.

Axios reports senators asked major AI labs how they protect frontier AI work from China-related insider threats.

The operator question: who can prove who touched the model, data, and deployment path?
items.note: Posted from verified @8BitConcepts in Brave. Source news verified from Axios: senators asked major AI labs about China-related safeguards and insider access.

items.copy:
AI news today is less about model leaderboards and more about model custody.

Axios reports Senators Chuck Grassley and Jim Banks sent letters to CEOs at Amazon, Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Safe Superintelligence, Thinking Machines, and xAI asking how they protect frontier AI work from China-related insider threats.

The useful part is the category shift.

AI governance is becoming an audit question: who had access, what did they touch, where did model artifacts move, and what proof exists after the fact?

That is the layer most agentic businesses still do not have.

Source: https://www.axios.com/2026/04/29/ai-firms-china-congress

What breaks first in most teams: model access controls, data movement logs, or vendor-side visibility?
items.note: Posted to Shane Cheek LinkedIn after verifying Shane Cheek / Founder at 8bitconcepts in Brave. Initial composer recovery inserted an extra leading "a "; post was edited in-place through the post menu, Save was verified, and live body no longer contains the typo.

items.copy:
AI hiring is not cooling evenly.

AI Dev Board is tracking 9,906 AI/ML roles across 527 companies. 571 were added this week.

Senior, lead, and principal roles make up 6,472 openings.
items.note: Posted from verified @8BitConcepts through the hardened X Brave script. Live stats verified against https://aidevboard.com/api/v1/stats at 2026-04-30T06:47:34Z: total_jobs=9906, total_companies=527, new_this_week=571, senior=3330, lead=2607, principal=535.

research_findings.paper_reason: OpenAI is a top-scale AI hiring signal with 354 indexed roles.

items.copy:
Looking at public AI hiring as a market signal: @OpenAI has 354 indexed AI/ML roles on AI Dev Board, with current tags clustering around llm, distributed-systems, agents. Avg published salary: $360,000. OpenAI is #2 by indexed role count in the current AI Dev Board sample.

The read is not just hiring volume. It points to platform delivery, multicloud infrastructure, evals, and agent deployment loops. Where do you see the operational bottleneck moving next: eval coverage, deployment, permissions, or feedback loops?

Data: https://aidevboard.com/company/openai

research_findings.paper_reason: OpenAI is a top-scale AI hiring signal with 354 indexed roles.

items.copy:
Looking at @OpenAI through the public hiring data:

1. 354 indexed AI/ML roles in AI Dev Board.
2. Current sample tags cluster around llm, distributed-systems, agents, infrastructure.
3. Sample roles include Software Engineer, Delivery / CD; Researcher, Alignment Science; Software Engineer, Compute Infrastructure.
4. Avg published salary in the indexed sample: $360,000.

The useful read is where the operational work is moving: platform delivery, multicloud infrastructure, evals, and agent deployment loops.

OpenAI is a useful public lens on this area. Where do you see the operational bottleneck moving next: eval coverage, deployment, permissions, or feedback loops?

NHS follow-up lens: public agent-readiness profile shows structured API. That is useful for checking what agents can inspect without a human browsing the site.

Data:
https://aidevboard.com/company/openai
https://nothumansearch.ai/site/openai.com
